title: Chapter 9: The Past Tense
content: Day three with Julian C. Rain, as always.

He's stronger today. I can tell by the way he moves between the counter and the tables — no sharp inhale when he bends, no pause before he lifts the kettle. He pours tea like someone who's done it a thousand mornings, the water hitting the cup in a clean arc, no splash. But I catch him. When he thinks I'm restocking, when he thinks no one's watching, he presses his palm flat against his side — against the wound — and his jaw sets like concrete. Just for a second. Then he drops his hand and goes back to being fine.
I don't call him on it.
The café has regulars now, and that's his fault.

Declan came first — an old fisherman with a face like driftwood, hands the size of dinner plates, and a stories-for-tea barter system that Julian C accepted without hesitation. Declan tells tales about the sea that no one under seventy believes and everyone over seventy swears are true. He sits in the corner booth every morning now, ordered and predictable as the tide, and Julian C keeps his cup full without being asked.

Then the young mother — I never got her name — who started coming two days ago because the café was quiet and her baby could sleep without being jostled by noise. She nurses a single Earl Grey for an hour and a half while her infant dozes in the pram, and Julian C always has a warm scone waiting when she looks up from her phone, bleary. He doesn't make conversation. He just makes space.

He's natural here. That's the thing I can't get past. This man — this fugitive, this stranger sleeping on a cot in a storage room — belongs in a café like a cat belongs in a sunbeam. He fits. He fills nooks and warms surfaces and makes this place run smoother than it ever has in any version of it I've inhabited. It makes no sense. It makes every kind of sense.

The morning passes the way mornings in Irish cafés pass: slowly, thickly, full of steam and salt air and the low murmur of people who've decided, for reasons they couldn't articulate, that this is where they want to be.

By afternoon, the lull settles in. Declan's gone. The young mother too. The rain has started again — it never really stops here, just changes volume — and the café empties to the sound of it hushing against the glass. I wipe down the counter. Julian C sits at the table by the window with my copy of Borges' *Labyrinths* open in his hands.

I didn't know this version of myself owned it. I found it on the shelf above the espresso machine two days ago, spine cracked, pages soft with handling. Someone's marginalia in pencil — neat, tiny handwriting that isn't mine. Or maybe it is. Maybe in this timeline I'm the kind of person who writes in margins. The thought makes me feel like I'm haunting my own life.
He turns a page. Then another. Then he stops.
"The Garden of Forking Paths," he says, not looking up.
I lean against the counter. "You've read it before?"

"Once. A long time ago." He traces a line of text with his fingertip, careful, like he's following a thread. Then he looks up at me. "You believe in this? That every choice creates a new world? That all possible futures exist simultaneously?"
The air in the café goes strange. Thin. Like the oxygen's been swapped for something sharper.
I almost can't breathe.

Because the question isn't philosophical. He's not asking about Borges. He's asking about something he senses — something he can't name but feels the shape of, the way you feel a wall in a dark room. And I'm standing on the other side of that wall with every timeline I've ever lived pressed against my chest like contraband.
"Maybe," I say. My voice comes out steadier than I am. "What about you?"

He closes the book. Gently — the way you close something that matters. His thumb rests on the cover for a moment, and when he looks at me again, his eyes are darker than they should be in this grey light.
"I believe some choices you can't undo. No matter how many paths fork off."

His voice is heavy. Not philosophical. Not abstract. Something he did. Or didn't do. The weight of it pulls at the corners of his mouth, and I watch him set it down between us like an unopened letter.
I don't push. I've learned, in three days, that Julian C doesn't unfold when you pull. He unfolds when you wait.
So I wait.

The rain fills the silence. I make more tea — Barry's, because this version of the café stocks it and because the smell of it steeping is the smell of this place, this timeline, this life I'm borrowing. I set a cup in front of him. He wraps both hands around it, the steam rising past his face, and for a long while he just looks at the surface of it like he's reading something there.
Then he talks.

"Not the discharge," he says. "That's the story everyone gets. The official one. Dishonorable discharge, insubordination, end of file." He turns the cup slowly between his palms. "But that's not what happened. I mean — it is. On paper. But it's not the thing."
I sit across from him. The chair creaks. The rain keeps tapping at the window like it wants in.

"My unit was deployed to extract a civilian from a conflict zone. Standard humanitarian op. The target was a teacher — local woman who'd been running an underground school for girls. When the region destabilized, she became a target. We were sent to get her out."
He takes a breath. It's steady, but I can see the cost of it in the way his shoulders tighten.
"Mid-mission, the orders changed. New directive from command: leave the civilian. Retrieve the asset."
"Asset?" I ask.
"Information. Documents. Something that was never clarified and that I never saw." His jaw works. "We were told the asset was priority. The teacher was... collateral. Expendable."
The word sits between us like a stone dropped in still water.

"I refused," he says. "I got the teacher out. Got her across the border to a refugee camp. She's alive because of it. Probably still teaching, somewhere, if she made it to a country that lets women teach."
He drinks from the cup. Sets it down.

"They court-martialed me for insubordination. That part's true. But then someone — I don't know who, I never found out — framed me for an information leak. Classified intel that supposedly went missing during the op. Only it never went missing. It never existed. The leak was fabricated to bury me. To make sure the story wasn't 'soldier disobeys sketchy order to save a life' but 'soldier jeopardizes mission, leaks intel, endangers unit.'"
He looks at me. His eyes are dry. That's worse than tears would be — it means he's been through this enough times that the well has run dry.
"So now I'm a fugitive," he says, "because I did the right thing."
The bitter irony in his voice could corrode metal. It could eat through the floor of this café and keep going, down through the foundation, all the way to the bedrock.

I should analyze. That's what I do. That's what Avery Chen does in every timeline — she catalogues, she strategizes, she computes the angles and determines the optimal next move. I should be running permutations right now: How does this change what I know about his pursuers? What does it tell me about the power structures hunting him? How does this affect the probability matrix of every Julian in every branch?
I don't.
I don't do any of that.
Instead, I reach across the table and take his hand.

It happens before I can think about it. My fingers find his, and they thread together like they've been doing it for years, and his hand is warm — rough-knuckled, calloused, with a scar across the back that I've never noticed before — and he goes completely still.

He looks down at our hands. At our intertwined fingers. He stares at them like he's never seen hands before. Like the concept of one person reaching for another is a foreign language he half-remembers from childhood.
"That's the stupidest brave thing I've ever heard," I say.

He almost laughs. Almost. The corner of his mouth lifts — just one side, just a fraction — and it's the most real expression I've seen on his face. Not the careful warmth he gives Declan. Not the quiet competence he wears behind the counter. This is something unguarded. Something that cracked open before he could catch it.
"You would think that," he says, and his voice is rough.
I don't let go of his hand. He doesn't pull away.

We sit like that until the rain shifts from a murmur to a full-throated downpour, and the grey afternoon darkens into early evening, and the café is just us and the sound of the sea throwing itself against the cliffs beyond the village.
When he finally lets go — to stand, to move — I feel the absence of his warmth like a word caught in my throat.

Evening comes the way it always does on this coast: suddenly, as if someone threw a blanket over the sky. The rain pounds the windows in sheets. Julian C goes to the glass and stands with his back to me, looking out at the churning water.
His body changes.

I don't know how else to describe it. The café worker evaporates. The man who pours tea and makes space for tired mothers and listens to Declan's stories — that man steps aside, and what's left is the soldier. His spine straightens. His weight shifts to the balls of his feet. His eyes track across the darkened street outside with a precision that makes my skin prickle. He's scanning. Assessing. Reading the terrain for threats with a speed and thoroughness that no amount of café work can train out of a person.

I see him, now, the way he must have looked on mission. The way he must have stood at checkpoints and in transport vehicles and in the seconds before everything went wrong. Capable. Alert. A weapon in human form.
"There's someone coming," he says.
I'm already on my feet. "Who?"
"A man named Harker." He doesn't turn from the window. "He traced me to Cork. I thought I'd lost him there, but the trail's too fresh. He'll have recalibrated. He'll be here within the week."
"Harker." I say the name like I'm testing its weight. "Who is he?"
"Someone who doesn't get paid unless I'm caught. Or dead. He's not picky about which."
Finally, he turns. His face is hard — not cruel, not cold, just closed in the way a door is closed. Deliberate. Necessary.
"I need to go before he finds you too."

There it is. The exit strategy. The selfless play. Julian C, doing the right thing again — warning me, protecting me, leaving before his trouble can contaminate this small safe harbor he's carved out of a café on the Irish coast.

My chest aches with the fairness of it. The wrongness of it. A man who saved a teacher's life and was destroyed for it, now running from the only person in any timeline who knows the truth, because he won't let his catastrophe become someone else's.
He's always doing the right thing. And it's always costing him everything.

I should agree. I know I should. I should nod and wish him well and shift back to Timeline A and never return to this path. That's the smart choice. The safe choice. The Avery Chen choice — the one I make in every branch, every world, every iteration of this life: calculate the risk, minimize the exposure, protect the primary variable.
The primary variable is me. It's always me.

But I'm looking at him — at this man who belongs in a café the way I belong in every world and none — and I can't make the calculus work. The numbers come out wrong. The answer isn't self-preservation. The answer isn't optimal distance. The answer is something my other selves wouldn't recognize, something that has nothing to do with the multiverse and everything to do with this room, this rain, this man whose hand I held half an hour ago and whose warmth I can still feel in my fingers.
"What if I don't want you to go?"

The words leave me before I can catch them. They hang in the air, stripped of strategy, stripped of the armor I've worn across a hundred timelines. Just me. Just Avery, asking a question that has no tactical upside and no safe answer.
His face does something complicated.

Fear first — sharp and immediate, the flinch of a man who's been bracing for betrayal so long that sincerity feels like a trap. Then hope, raw and reluctant, like something that's been beaten down so many times it's learned to stay small. And underneath both — underneath everything — a hunger so raw it frightens me.

Not sexual. Deeper than that. The hunger of someone who's been alone so long he's forgotten what it feels like to be asked to stay. The hunger of a man standing at a door that's never been opened for him, watching it swing wide and not trusting that it won't slam shut on his fingers.
He doesn't answer.

He just holds my gaze — steady, unblinking, the soldier and the café worker and the man who saved a stranger because it was right, all of them looking back at me at once — and the rain fills the silence, and Harker is coming, and neither of us moves.
